Questions: What is Alexander Elder's 2% Rule?
Answers: The 2% Rule states that a trader must never risk more than 2% of their total account equity on any single trade. Risk is defined as the dollar difference between the entry price and the stop-loss level multiplied by the number of shares — not the total value of the position.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder define 'risk' in the 2% Rule?
Answers: Risk is not the total cost of the position. It is the dollar amount you could lose if the trade is stopped out — calculated as the distance from the entry price to the stop-loss price, multiplied by the number of shares or contracts traded.
Label: Risk Management
Source: A Discounted View – Managing Position Level Risk with Elder's 2% Rule | http://adiscountedview.blogspot.com/2016/03/managing-position-level-risk-with-dr.html

Questions: Why does the 2% Rule use account equity rather than position value?
Answers: Using account equity rather than position value links risk directly to what the trader can actually afford to lose. A position could be worth many times 2% of an account, but as long as the stop-loss limits the potential loss to 2%, the rule is satisfied.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: How do you calculate the maximum number of shares using the 2% Rule?
Answers: First, calculate 2% of account equity to get the maximum dollar risk. Then divide that amount by the per-share risk (entry price minus stop-loss price). The result is the maximum number of shares you may buy. For example: $20,000 account × 2% = $400 maximum risk. Entry at $30, stop at $28 = $2 risk per share. $400 ÷ $2 = 200 shares maximum.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: What is a worked example of the 2% Rule on a $100,000 account?
Answers: On a $100,000 account, 2% equals $2,000 maximum risk per trade. If a trader buys a stock at $30 and places a stop at $26, the risk per share is $4. Dividing $2,000 by $4 gives a maximum position size of 500 shares.
Label: Risk Management
Source: A Discounted View – Managing Position Level Risk with Elder's 2% Rule | http://adiscountedview.blogspot.com/2016/03/managing-position-level-risk-with-dr.html

Questions: What is a worked example of the 2% Rule on a $20,000 account?
Answers: On a $20,000 account, 2% is $400 maximum risk. If the entry is $30 and the stop is $28 — a $2 risk per share — the trader may buy at most 200 shares. Fewer shares are acceptable; the 2% calculation sets the ceiling, not a requirement.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: Does the 2% Rule require a trader to always risk exactly 2%?
Answers: No. The 2% Rule sets a maximum, not a requirement. Traders are perfectly welcome to risk less — for example, 1% or even 0.5% per trade. Elder specifically states that most professional traders risk well below the 2% ceiling, closer to 1% per position.
Label: Risk Management
Source: A Discounted View – Managing Position Level Risk with Elder's 2% Rule | http://adiscountedview.blogspot.com/2016/03/managing-position-level-risk-with-dr.html

Questions: What does Elder say about professional traders and the 2% Rule?
Answers: Elder notes that most professional traders risk even less than 2% per trade. He explicitly states: 'Good traders tend to stay well below the 2% limit. Try to risk less than 2% — it is simply the maximum level.' The 2% level is an absolute ceiling, not a target.
Label: Risk Management
Source: A Discounted View – Managing Position Level Risk with Elder's 2% Rule | http://adiscountedview.blogspot.com/2016/03/managing-position-level-risk-with-dr.html

Questions: What problem does the 2% Rule specifically protect against?
Answers: The 2% Rule protects against a single catastrophic loss — what Elder calls a 'shark bite.' One oversized position that blows up can wipe out months or even years of accumulated gains. The 2% cap ensures no single trade can deal a fatal blow to the account.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: How does the 2% Rule change with different account sizes?
Answers: The dollar amount changes proportionally, but the percentage principle is constant. A $10,000 account risks a maximum of $200 per trade. A $50,000 account risks $1,000. A $200,000 account risks $4,000. Elder notes that larger account holders tend to risk an even lower percentage because large absolute dollar losses create disproportionate psychological pressure.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: Can the 2% Rule be adjusted for higher-risk traders?
Answers: Elder generally recommends that the 2% Rule is used as an absolute maximum. However, some authors note that aggressive traders may adapt the framework to a 5% per-trade maximum — though Elder himself discourages this, emphasizing that capital preservation is paramount for long-term survival.
Label: Risk Management
Source: SteadyOptions – How Position Sizing Impacts Your Returns | https://steadyoptions.com/articles/position-sizing/

Questions: Why is it important NOT to confuse the 2% risk with 2% of position size?
Answers: Confusing risk with position size is a critical error. A trader could hold a position worth 30–40% of their account and still satisfy the 2% Rule, provided the stop-loss limits the potential loss to 2% of equity. The 2% refers exclusively to the downside exposure, not the capital committed.
Label: Risk Management
Source: A Discounted View – Managing Position Level Risk with Elder's 2% Rule | http://adiscountedview.blogspot.com/2016/03/managing-position-level-risk-with-dr.html

Questions: What happens if a trader violates the 2% Rule?
Answers: Violating the 2% Rule exposes the account to a loss large enough to impair the trader's ability to recover. Elder compares oversized losses to a shark bite — one hit can take a trader permanently out of the market. Even one violation can undo weeks or months of disciplined gains.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does the 2% Rule help during a losing streak?
Answers: By capping each loss at 2%, a series of consecutive losing trades produces predictable, manageable drawdowns. Ten consecutive losses at 2% risk would deplete the account by roughly 18%, leaving 82% of equity intact and a realistic path to recovery — compared to a 50% or greater drawdown from oversized trades.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Grokipedia – The New Trading for a Living | https://grokipedia.com/page/the_new_trading_for_a_living_psychology_discipline_trading_tools_and_systems_risk_control_trade_mana

Questions: What role does the stop-loss play in the 2% Rule?
Answers: The stop-loss is the mechanism that enforces the 2% Rule. Without a stop-loss, risk is theoretically unlimited, because the position could fall to zero. The stop-loss converts an open-ended risk into a defined, capped exposure that can be calculated and controlled.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How should a trader set the stop-loss level before applying the 2% Rule?
Answers: The stop-loss level should be set first based on technical analysis — at a logical level below support for a long trade — and then the 2% Rule is applied to calculate the correct position size. The technical level drives the stop; the 2% Rule then determines how many shares to buy.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about writing down entry, target, and stop before every trade?
Answers: Elder insists that every trade requires three numbers to be written down before placement: entry, target, and stop. Without all three, a trade becomes a gamble. The stop in particular is non-negotiable — it defines risk, and without it, proper position sizing is impossible.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: Why does the 2% Rule require the stop to be set in advance rather than after entry?
Answers: Setting the stop before entry is the only moment of complete objectivity. Once a trader is in a position, fear or hope distorts judgment. A pre-set stop removes the emotional temptation to widen it later when the trade goes against you.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Ticker Tape / TD Ameritrade – How the 2% and 6% Rules Can Help | https://tickertape.tdameritrade.com/trading/rules-to-manage-risk-15908

Questions: What is the relationship between account size and the 2% Rule?
Answers: As account size grows, the absolute dollar risk per trade increases, but the percentage stays fixed at 2% or less. Elder recommends that larger accounts actually use a lower percentage — since large dollar amounts create greater psychological pressure and can lead to impaired decision-making.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does the 2% Rule support long-term survival in trading?
Answers: By keeping losses small and proportional to equity, the 2% Rule ensures the trader always has enough capital remaining to take the next trade. Elder compares this to a scuba diver monitoring their air supply — surviving to trade another day is more important than any single trade outcome.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: What is the Iron Triangle of Risk Control?
Answers: The Iron Triangle is Elder's visual framework for position sizing. Point A equals the maximum dollar risk (2% of account equity). Point B equals the risk per share (entry price minus stop-loss price). Point C — the number of shares to trade — equals A divided by B. All three points are fixed by calculation, not guesswork.
Label: Risk Management
Source: StockCharts ChartWatchers – Dr Elder Joins ChartWatchers | https://stockcharts.com/articles/chartwatchers/2010/05/dr-alexander-elder-joins-chartwatchers.html

Questions: How did Elder illustrate the Iron Triangle in a real trade?
Answers: In a trade on the gold stock AUY, Elder decided to risk $500 (his maximum for that week). His initial plan had a $0.24 risk per share (entry $9.85, stop $9.61), allowing 2,000 shares. When he entered at $9.79 instead, the risk per share dropped to $0.18. Dividing $500 by $0.18 allowed him to increase the position to 3,000 shares — demonstrating how the Iron Triangle automatically adjusts position size when entry improves.
Label: Risk Management
Source: StockCharts ChartWatchers – Dr Elder Joins ChartWatchers | https://stockcharts.com/articles/chartwatchers/2010/05/dr-alexander-elder-joins-chartwatchers.html

Questions: What does Elder mean when he says the 2% Rule is the most important rule of risk control?
Answers: Elder writes that the most important rule of risk control is the 2% Rule because without it, a single bad trade can remove a trader from the market permanently. All other tools — indicators, systems, psychology — are irrelevant if one position wipes out the account before they can be applied.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about beginners and risk calculation?
Answers: Elder observes that most beginners spend zero time calculating and managing risk. Just before placing a trade, they may impulsively double their usual size after a win or skip a trade after a loss. Both choices are equally wrong because they are emotional rather than mathematical, and neither follows a consistent risk framework.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: How does the 2% Rule compare to a stop on an individual trade?
Answers: Elder explicitly compares the 2% Rule to placing a stop-loss on a single position. Just as a stop limits the damage any one trade can do to a position, the 2% Rule limits the damage any one trade can do to the entire account.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: How do brokerage commissions interact with the 2% Rule?
Answers: Elder notes that some practitioners of the 2% Rule include an allowance for brokerage commissions and slippage within the 2% calculation. The logic is that commissions are a real cost of the trade and should be counted as part of the total risk exposure, not treated as a separate expense.
Label: Risk Management
Source: BWTS Ask Colin – Could you explain the 6% Rule | https://www.bwts.com.au/resources/ask-colin/817-could-you-explain-the-6-rule-as-opposed-to-the-2-rule-and-sugges.html

Questions: Is the 2% Rule applicable to all markets — stocks, futures, forex, options?
Answers: Yes. Elder designed the 2% Rule as a universal capital protection principle applicable across all trading vehicles — stocks, futures, options, forex, and CFDs. The calculation methodology is identical; only the unit of risk (per share, per contract, per lot) differs by market.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does proper position sizing from the 2% Rule change total portfolio returns?
Answers: Position sizing has a dramatic effect on total returns. A trade with a small average loss but large allocation can generate far higher portfolio returns than a trade with a higher win rate but poor sizing. Elder's framework ensures that high-conviction trades can be sized appropriately while maintaining hard risk limits.
Label: Risk Management
Source: SteadyOptions – How Position Sizing Impacts Your Returns | https://steadyoptions.com/articles/position-sizing/

Questions: What is the key insight that separates the 2% Rule from simply 'trading smaller'?
Answers: The 2% Rule is not a blanket instruction to trade smaller. It is a dynamic framework that scales position size to both account equity and the specific risk of each trade. A tight stop allows a larger position; a wide stop requires a smaller one. This makes the rule adaptive rather than rigid.
Label: Risk Management
Source: A Discounted View – Managing Position Level Risk with Elder's 2% Rule | http://adiscountedview.blogspot.com/2016/03/managing-position-level-risk-with-dr.html

Questions: Why does Elder say keeping risk 'small and constant' is absolutely critical?
Answers: Keeping risk small and constant means the trader's performance is driven entirely by the quality of their trading decisions — not by random variation in position sizes. Inconsistent sizing introduces noise that makes it impossible to evaluate whether a system is truly working or simply benefiting from one lucky oversized bet.
Label: Risk Management
Source: A Discounted View – Managing Position Level Risk with Elder's 2% Rule | http://adiscountedview.blogspot.com/2016/03/managing-position-level-risk-with-dr.html

Questions: What is Alexander Elder's 6% Rule?
Answers: The 6% Rule states that a trader must stop opening any new positions for the remainder of the calendar month as soon as the combined total of realized losses and open risk in current positions reaches 6% of account equity at the start of that month.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does the 6% Rule complement the 2% Rule?
Answers: The 2% Rule protects against a single catastrophic loss (the shark bite). The 6% Rule protects against a series of smaller losses that collectively devastate the account (the piranha bites). Together they form a two-level defense — trade-level protection and portfolio-level protection.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: What is Elder's 'shark and piranha' metaphor for risk management?
Answers: Elder writes that markets can kill a trading account in two ways: a single devastating loss like a shark bite, or a series of small losses like piranha bites — none of them lethal individually but together capable of shredding an account to the bone. The 2% Rule stops the sharks; the 6% Rule stops the piranhas.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: How is the 6% Rule calculated in practice?
Answers: At the start of each month, note the account equity. Add up all realized losses incurred during the month plus the current open risk (potential loss if all stops are hit) on all open positions. When this sum reaches 6% of the starting equity, stop trading immediately and open no new positions until the next month begins.
Label: Risk Management
Source: A Discounted View – Minimizing Portfolio Risk with Elder's 6% Rule | http://adiscountedview.blogspot.com/2016/03/minimizing-portfolio-risk-with-dr.html

Questions: What is a worked example of the 6% Rule on a $50,000 account?
Answers: On a $50,000 account, 6% equals $3,000. If a trader has already realized $1,000 in losses this month and has three open trades each with $500 in open risk, the total exposure is $1,000 + $1,500 = $2,500. Adding one more trade with $500 risk would bring the total to $3,000, triggering the 6% Rule and requiring all new trading to stop for the rest of the month.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Four Minute Books – The New Trading for a Living Summary | https://fourminutebooks.com/the-new-trading-for-a-living-summary/

Questions: What is a worked example of the 6% Rule on a $100,000 account?
Answers: On a $100,000 account, 6% equals $6,000. If four losing trades of $1,000 each have been realized ($4,000 total) and two open trades each carry $1,000 in open risk ($2,000), the combined exposure is $6,000 — exactly at the limit. No new trades may be opened for the rest of the month.
Label: Risk Management
Source: A Discounted View – Minimizing Portfolio Risk with Elder's 6% Rule | http://adiscountedview.blogspot.com/2016/03/minimizing-portfolio-risk-with-dr.html

Questions: Why does the 6% Rule include open risk, not just realized losses?
Answers: Including open risk in the 6% calculation prevents a trader from technically being below the 6% threshold in realized losses while simultaneously carrying enough open risk to exceed it if all stops are hit simultaneously. Elder's framework is forward-looking — it accounts for the worst plausible outcome, not just what has already happened.
Label: Risk Management
Source: A Discounted View – Minimizing Portfolio Risk with Elder's 6% Rule | http://adiscountedview.blogspot.com/2016/03/minimizing-portfolio-risk-with-dr.html

Questions: How does the 6% Rule act as a 'stop-loss on the account'?
Answers: Just as a stop-loss on a single trade defines the maximum acceptable loss on that position and closes it automatically, the 6% Rule places a stop on the entire account — defining the maximum acceptable monthly drawdown and halting all new trading when it is reached.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: What should a trader do when the 6% Rule is triggered?
Answers: When the 6% threshold is reached, Elder says the trader must stop opening new positions for the rest of the month. Some traders switch to paper trading or simulated trading to stay engaged with the market without real capital exposure. Others use the forced pause to review their journal and diagnose why the losing streak occurred.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Ticker Tape / TD Ameritrade – How the 2% and 6% Rules Can Help | https://tickertape.tdameritrade.com/trading/rules-to-manage-risk-15908

Questions: Why does the 6% Rule work against human nature?
Answers: As traders experience mounting losses, their instinct is to trade more often or in larger size to recover the losses quickly. The 6% Rule directly opposes this impulse by forcing a complete stop when losses accumulate — preventing the revenge trading cycle that typically accelerates drawdowns rather than reversing them.
Label: Risk Management
Source: A Discounted View – Minimizing Portfolio Risk with Elder's 6% Rule | http://adiscountedview.blogspot.com/2016/03/minimizing-portfolio-risk-with-dr.html

Questions: What does 'revenge trading' mean and how does the 6% Rule prevent it?
Answers: Revenge trading is the compulsive urge to immediately place new trades to recover recent losses — driven by frustration and the need to 'get even' with the market. The 6% Rule mechanically prevents this by removing the option to trade once the monthly threshold is hit, giving the trader a mandatory cooling-off period.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about a series of small losses being as dangerous as one large loss?
Answers: Elder explains that a single 1% loss seems trivial, but five or more of them within one month accumulate into a meaningful financial and psychological setback. A series of small losses can be just as destructive as one large one — and the 6% Rule is specifically designed to interrupt such streaks before they become catastrophic.
Label: Risk Management
Source: A Discounted View – Minimizing Portfolio Risk with Elder's 6% Rule | http://adiscountedview.blogspot.com/2016/03/minimizing-portfolio-risk-with-dr.html

Questions: What psychological benefit does the 6% Rule provide?
Answers: The 6% Rule provides psychological relief by removing the agonising decision of 'should I keep trading or stop?' from the trader's hands. Once the threshold is reached, the rule makes the decision automatically. This removes the burden of willpower and replaces it with a clear, pre-committed rule.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: Can a trader with a very low risk-per-trade benefit from the 6% Rule?
Answers: Yes. If a trader risks only 1% per trade instead of 2%, the 6% Rule allows them to take up to six losing trades before being stopped out for the month — providing more latitude to practice and refine tactics compared to a trader using the full 2% maximum per trade.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: Why does Elder recommend lowering the 2% maximum to 1% for beginners?
Answers: Reducing maximum risk per trade to 1% doubles the number of losing trades a beginner can absorb before hitting the 6% monthly cap. This gives beginners more opportunities to learn, make mistakes, and adjust their approach without being forced out of the market early in the month.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: How does the 6% Rule contribute to long-term account preservation?
Answers: By capping the maximum monthly drawdown at 6%, the 6% Rule ensures that even the worst months do not permanently impair the account. A 6% drawdown requires only a 6.4% gain to recover — a manageable target. Without the 6% Rule, unchecked losing streaks can create drawdowns that require impossibly large returns to recover.
Label: Risk Management
Source: A Discounted View – Minimizing Portfolio Risk with Elder's 6% Rule | http://adiscountedview.blogspot.com/2016/03/minimizing-portfolio-risk-with-dr.html

Questions: What is the difference between a realized loss and open risk in the 6% calculation?
Answers: A realized loss is money already gone — a closed trade that ended at a loss. Open risk is the potential loss still outstanding on currently open positions if all stops are triggered simultaneously. The 6% Rule requires both to be counted, because open risk is just as real as a realized loss from a capital preservation standpoint.
Label: Risk Management
Source: BWTS Ask Colin – Could you explain the 6% Rule | https://www.bwts.com.au/resources/ask-colin/817-could-you-explain-the-6-rule-as-opposed-to-the-2-rule-and-sugges.html

Questions: How does the 6% Rule interact with having multiple simultaneous open positions?
Answers: If a trader has three open positions each with $500 in open risk (total $1,500 in open risk), those $1,500 count toward the 6% monthly limit even before any position is closed at a loss. This prevents the trap of accumulating multiple positions that each seem small individually but collectively represent excessive portfolio risk.
Label: Risk Management
Source: A Discounted View – Minimizing Portfolio Risk with Elder's 6% Rule | http://adiscountedview.blogspot.com/2016/03/minimizing-portfolio-risk-with-dr.html

Questions: What does Elder compare the 6% Rule's protective function to in a trade context?
Answers: Elder compares the 6% Rule directly to a stop-loss order, but applied at the account level rather than the trade level. Just as a stop-loss limits how much damage one position can do to the portfolio, the 6% Rule limits how much damage an entire month's trading can do to the account.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: How do the 2% and 6% Rules together bound the maximum number of positions?
Answers: If every trade risks the maximum 2%, the 6% Rule allows a maximum of three simultaneous open trades (3 × 2% = 6%). If risk is reduced to 1% per trade, up to six trades can be open simultaneously. The interaction between the two rules automatically limits position count without needing a separate rule for it.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is an alternative version of the 2% and 6% Rules for higher-risk tolerance?
Answers: Some practitioners adjust the framework to a 5% per-trade maximum and a 10% monthly maximum for traders with higher risk tolerance. However, Elder himself does not recommend this escalation — he views the original 2% and 6% levels as the appropriate boundaries for sustainable trading.
Label: Risk Management
Source: SteadyOptions – How Position Sizing Impacts Your Returns | https://steadyoptions.com/articles/position-sizing/

Questions: What does Elder say about how a series of 1% losses feels financially versus psychologically?
Answers: Elder acknowledges that a single 1% loss feels innocuous — almost too small to worry about. But he warns that five or more such losses accumulating within one month begin to affect the trader both financially and psychologically, creating self-doubt, second-guessing, and a deterioration of decision quality that can lead to even larger losses.
Label: Risk Management
Source: A Discounted View – Minimizing Portfolio Risk with Elder's 6% Rule | http://adiscountedview.blogspot.com/2016/03/minimizing-portfolio-risk-with-dr.html

Questions: How does the 6% Rule force the trader to reassess their system?
Answers: When the 6% limit is hit, Elder says this is a signal that the trader's method is not working in the current market environment. The forced pause is an opportunity — not just a punishment — to step back, review the trade journal, identify what has changed, and diagnose whether the problem is the system, the execution, or the market conditions.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say should happen after the 6% Rule triggers and the new month begins?
Answers: Elder recommends returning to trading at the start of the new month with reduced position sizes — potentially cutting risk per trade to 1% or lower — to rebuild confidence and test whether the system is back in gear with current market conditions before returning to full size.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does the 6% Rule differ from simply deciding 'I'll stop trading if I have a bad month'?
Answers: An informal personal decision to stop trading after bad months is subject to emotional override — the trader may rationalize continuing because the next trade 'feels' like a sure winner. The 6% Rule is a pre-committed, mechanical threshold that removes discretion entirely. Because it was decided before the losing streak began, it cannot be rationalised away in the heat of the moment.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Financial Wisdom TV – Trading for a Living by Dr Alexander Elder | https://www.financialwisdomtv.com/post/trading-for-a-living

Questions: What is the primary purpose of a stop-loss order in Elder's framework?
Answers: A stop-loss order serves two purposes simultaneously: it caps the maximum financial loss on a trade to the amount calculated under the 2% Rule, and it removes the emotional decision of whether to exit during a losing trade by automating the exit at a pre-determined level.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: Where does Elder place stop-losses for long positions?
Answers: For long positions, Elder places the stop-loss one tick below the low of the current bar or the previous bar — whichever is lower. This placement keeps the stop below any recently established price support while being tight enough to cap risk within the 2% limit.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: Where does Elder place stop-losses for short positions?
Answers: For short positions, Elder places the stop-loss one tick above the high of the current bar or the previous bar — whichever is higher — mirroring the same logic used for long stops but applied to the upside.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: Why must the stop-loss be set before the trade is entered?
Answers: Before entering a trade is the only moment the trader has complete objectivity. Once in a position, fear of loss and hope of recovery cloud judgment. A pre-set stop was decided when the mind was clear and unemotional — making it a far more rational exit level than any stop set after the position is open and the trader is under stress.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Ticker Tape / TD Ameritrade – How the 2% and 6% Rules Can Help | https://tickertape.tdameritrade.com/trading/rules-to-manage-risk-15908

Questions: What does Elder mean by 'say no to wishful thinking' when setting stops?
Answers: Elder dedicates an entire section of The New Trading for a Living to this concept. Setting stops based on wishful thinking — placing them just wide enough to avoid being hit, rather than at a logical technical level — defeats the entire purpose of risk management. Stops must be placed based on market structure, not on what the trader hopes the market will do.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is the rule for moving stop-losses once a trade is open?
Answers: Stops should only be moved in the direction of the trade — tightened to lock in profits as the trade moves in the trader's favour. They must never be moved further away from the entry to give a losing trade more room. Widening a stop is an emotional decision that violates the pre-committed risk discipline.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: Why does Elder say widening a stop after entry is a form of self-destruction?
Answers: Widening a stop after entry is the trader's emotional refusal to accept that the trade is wrong. It converts a pre-planned, rational exit rule into an improvised, emotion-driven one. It also violates the 2% Rule's integrity by allowing losses to grow beyond the originally calculated maximum.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is a trailing stop and how does Elder use it?
Answers: A trailing stop is a stop-loss that is moved progressively in the direction of a profitable trade to lock in accumulated gains. Elder uses trailing stops to let winners run while protecting the profit that has been built — moving the stop up under each new higher low in an uptrend, for example.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is the SafeZone stop method Elder introduced in Come Into My Trading Room?
Answers: The SafeZone stop is Elder's volatility-adjusted stop placement method. It measures the typical 'noise' in a security's price movement — the normal day-to-day counter-trend fluctuations — and places stops beyond that noise level, so the trader is not stopped out by routine fluctuation but is protected when genuine trend reversal begins.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Grokipedia – The New Trading for a Living | https://grokipedia.com/page/the_new_trading_for_a_living_psychology_discipline_trading_tools_and_systems_risk_control_trade_mana

Questions: How does Average True Range (ATR) factor into Elder's stop placement?
Answers: Elder recommends using the ATR to set stops in proportion to a security's actual volatility. For long positions, an initial stop can be placed at the entry price minus 2 × ATR, providing a buffer against normal market fluctuations while still capping the downside. Targets can be set at 3 × ATR or more for a favourable risk-reward ratio.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Grokipedia – The New Trading for a Living | https://grokipedia.com/page/the_new_trading_for_a_living_psychology_discipline_trading_tools_and_systems_risk_control_trade_mana

Questions: Why does Elder prefer not to place stops at obvious round numbers?
Answers: Obvious round-number price levels ($50, $100) attract clusters of stop orders from many traders. Professional market makers are aware of these clusters and may deliberately push price briefly through them to trigger the stops and collect liquidity before reversing. Elder prefers stops placed slightly beyond these levels to avoid this 'stop hunting.'
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does stop placement interact with the 2% Rule when the technical stop is very wide?
Answers: When a technically justified stop is very wide (far from entry), the 2% Rule will force a very small position size. This is the correct outcome — a wide stop means higher per-share risk, so fewer shares must be bought to keep the total dollar risk at or below 2% of equity. The system automatically scales position size down for riskier setups.
Label: Risk Management
Source: A Discounted View – Managing Position Level Risk with Elder's 2% Rule | http://adiscountedview.blogspot.com/2016/03/managing-position-level-risk-with-dr.html

Questions: What does Elder say about stop-losses becoming market orders when triggered?
Answers: Elder notes that once a stop-loss price is reached, the order becomes a market order and competes with all other orders for execution. This means the fill may be slightly worse than the stop price, particularly in fast-moving or illiquid markets — a phenomenon called slippage. Traders should account for this in their risk calculations.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Ticker Tape / TD Ameritrade – How the 2% and 6% Rules Can Help | https://tickertape.tdameritrade.com/trading/rules-to-manage-risk-15908

Questions: How should a trader set stop-losses differently in high-volatility versus low-volatility environments?
Answers: In high-volatility environments, stops must be wider to accommodate larger price swings — meaning position sizes must be correspondingly smaller to keep dollar risk within the 2% cap. In low-volatility environments, tighter stops allow larger positions while maintaining the same dollar risk limit.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Grokipedia – The New Trading for a Living | https://grokipedia.com/page/the_new_trading_for_a_living_psychology_discipline_trading_tools_and_systems_risk_control_trade_mana

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological challenge of setting and honouring stops?
Answers: Elder identifies the refusal to honour a stop as one of the most common and expensive psychological failures in trading. It is rooted in ego — the inability to admit the trade is wrong. He teaches that accepting a small, pre-planned loss is not a failure but a routine operating cost of professional trading.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder mean when he says every trade needs a 'protective stop'?
Answers: A protective stop is Elder's term for the stop-loss order placed at entry specifically to protect the trader's capital. The word 'protective' emphasises its purpose — it is not an afterthought but the primary defence against the market moving violently against the position.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Financial Wisdom TV – Trading for a Living by Dr Alexander Elder | https://www.financialwisdomtv.com/post/trading-for-a-living

Questions: What are 'kangaroo tails' and how do they relate to stop placement?
Answers: Kangaroo tails — Elder's term for single very tall bars that protrude sharply from a tight cluster of prices — often signal trend reversals. A trader who holds a position through a kangaroo tail without a stop risks a sudden large adverse move. Elder uses them both as entry signals (false breakout reversals) and as warnings that existing stops may need to be tightened.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: How does Elder's view of support and resistance affect stop placement?
Answers: Elder teaches that support and resistance are not rigid walls but flexible zones — like a wire fence on a farm rather than a pane of glass. This means stops placed exactly at support levels may be triggered by minor violations before the level holds. Elder prefers stops placed slightly beyond the support zone to absorb normal false breakouts.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: Why does Elder say holding a trade through an earnings report is gambling, not trading?
Answers: Earnings reports introduce binary uncertainty that cannot be controlled with a stop-loss — the gap up or down at open can be far beyond the stop level. Elder teaches that holding through an earnings announcement converts a disciplined trade into a gamble with undefined risk, which directly violates the principles of the 2% Rule.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: What is the difference between a stop-loss and a profit target in Elder's framework?
Answers: A stop-loss protects capital by capping downside risk. A profit target protects gains by defining when 'enough is enough.' Elder treats both as pre-committed decisions made before entry — the stop defines the maximum acceptable loss; the target defines the acceptable gain — together bounding the trade's risk-reward profile.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the word 'enough' in relation to profit targets?
Answers: Elder calls 'enough' the power word when setting profit targets. He teaches that traders who chase the last bit of profit in a move inevitably give back gains trying to squeeze out more than the market intends to give. Defining a realistic profit target in advance — and exiting when it is reached — is a key discipline of professional risk management.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder recommend adjusting stop-losses as a trade moves in favour?
Answers: Once a trade is profitable, Elder recommends progressively trailing the stop to lock in gains — moving it up under each new higher low in an uptrend, for example. This converts a trade with defined downside risk into one that has a guaranteed minimum profit as the stop moves above the entry price.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Internet Archive – The New Sell and Sell Short (Elder) | https://archive.org/details/newsellsellshort0000elde

Questions: What does Elder say about adding to winning positions and stop management?
Answers: Elder approaches adding to winning positions cautiously. Each additional trade must have its own stop-loss placed at a technically appropriate level, and the combined risk of the entire enlarged position must still satisfy the 2% Rule — meaning the total dollar risk on all portions cannot exceed 2% of account equity.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder recommend for stops when using the Force Index?
Answers: Elder uses a two-day EMA of the Force Index to manage stops on open positions. In a weekly uptrend, he adds to long positions when the Force Index turns negative (a short-term pullback) and raises his stop when the Force Index recovers — using the indicator's rhythm to trail the stop systematically rather than arbitrarily.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is Elder's instruction for exiting a position when the Impulse System changes colour?
Answers: When the Impulse System bar transitions from green or blue to red — indicating that both the EMA slope and MACD-Histogram are falling — Elder treats this as a signal to exit long positions. The colour change provides a systematic, rule-based exit trigger that does not rely on discretion or emotional judgment.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Grokipedia – The New Trading for a Living | https://grokipedia.com/page/the_new_trading_for_a_living_psychology_discipline_trading_tools_and_systems_risk_control_trade_mana

Questions: What does Elder say about using limit orders for entry versus market orders for stops?
Answers: Elder almost always uses limit orders for entries to control slippage and ensure a favourable price. However, when a stop-loss is triggered, he switches to market orders to guarantee exit — because when a trade is going against you, getting out quickly is more important than saving a few ticks on the exit price.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How do false breakouts affect stop placement strategy?
Answers: False breakouts — brief excursions beyond support or resistance that quickly reverse — are among the most common causes of stop-outs in otherwise good trades. Elder responds by placing stops just beyond the range of recent price noise rather than directly at support/resistance lines, giving the position room to absorb brief false moves.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: What is Elder's view on the 'stop hunt' phenomenon?
Answers: Elder is aware that professional traders and market makers monitor clusters of stop orders at obvious technical levels. A brief push through a key level can trigger these stops and provide liquidity before the market reverses. His response is to place stops at slightly unconventional levels — away from round numbers and obvious clustering points.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder's stop placement philosophy reflect his view of trading as a profession?
Answers: Elder teaches that placing and honouring stops is what distinguishes professional traders from gamblers. Gamblers hold positions indefinitely, hoping for reversal. Professionals pre-define their maximum acceptable loss before entry and exit without hesitation when that level is reached — treating each stop-out as a routine business expense, not a personal failure.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Financial Wisdom TV – Trading for a Living by Dr Alexander Elder | https://www.financialwisdomtv.com/post/trading-for-a-living

Questions: What does Elder mean by 'a stop on the account' versus 'a stop on a trade'?
Answers: A stop on a trade (the stop-loss order) limits damage from any single position. A stop on the account (the 6% Rule) limits damage across all positions in a given month. Together, they create a two-tiered risk architecture: the trade-level stop prevents individual disasters; the account-level stop prevents cumulative disasters.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: Why does Elder consider record-keeping a component of risk management?
Answers: Elder places record-keeping alongside the 2% and 6% Rules as part of the Iron Triangle of risk control because without records, a trader cannot know whether their system is working, whether losses are within expected parameters, or whether a dangerous pattern of behaviour is developing. Records are the feedback loop that makes all other risk controls functional.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder's trader spreadsheet track beyond profit and loss?
Answers: Elder's trader spreadsheet tracks entry date, exit date, entry price, exit price, commissions, exchange fees, gross and net P&L, the source of the trade idea, the method used to find the trade, and — crucially — Buy Grade, Sell Grade, and Trade Grade for every trade.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: What is the Buy Grade in Elder's record-keeping system?
Answers: The Buy Grade measures the quality of the entry: (High of entry day − Buy price) ÷ (High − Low of entry day). A score above 50% means the trader bought in the lower half of the day's range — an excellent entry. Consistently low Buy Grades reveal that the trader is chasing prices rather than waiting for value.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: What is the Sell Grade in Elder's record-keeping system?
Answers: The Sell Grade measures the quality of the exit: (Sell price − Low of exit day) ÷ (High − Low of exit day). A score above 50% means the trader sold in the upper half of the day's range. Consistently low Sell Grades indicate the trader is panicking out at the bottom rather than exiting at strength.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: What is the Trade Grade in Elder's record-keeping system?
Answers: The Trade Grade evaluates the overall quality of a trade: (Sell price − Buy price) ÷ (Upper channel line − Lower channel line at entry). This measures what percentage of the available price channel the trader actually captured. A Trade Grade above 30% qualifies as an A-quality trade.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the link between good records and good trading?
Answers: Elder states explicitly: 'Show me a trader with good records and I'll show you a good trader. Show me a trader with poor or non-existent records and I'll show you an average trader — and an average trader loses money.' He views record-keeping as the single clearest predictor of long-term trading success.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: What does Elder mean by a 'visual diary' of trades?
Answers: A visual diary consists of saved chart screenshots taken on the day of entry and the day of exit, annotated with the trader's reasoning and observations at the time. Reviewing these visual records reveals patterns — what setups actually worked, what exit behaviour was costing profit — in a way that numbers alone cannot show.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: How does the personal equity curve function as a risk management tool?
Answers: The personal equity curve plots account value over time. A rising equity curve confirms the trading system is working. A declining or sideways equity curve is an early warning signal that the system is underperforming — prompting the trader to reduce size, pause, or investigate the cause of deterioration before losses compound.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does a downtrend in the personal equity curve tell a trader?
Answers: A downtrend in the equity curve tells the trader that something has changed — either the market environment no longer fits the system, the trader's execution has deteriorated, or a psychological issue is causing rule violations. Elder says the correct response is to reduce position size or stop trading until the cause is identified and corrected.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is Elder's rule 'it is OK to make mistakes, but not OK to repeat them'?
Answers: This rule, from Step by Step Trading, encapsulates why records are essential. A trader who does not document their trades has no way of knowing whether they are repeating the same mistakes. Records transform trading experience into structured learning — making each mistake a one-time tuition payment rather than a recurring cost.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: Why does Elder compare good record-keeping to weight control?
Answers: Elder writes that record-keeping is comparable to tracking your weight. If you do not know how much you weigh and how much you are gaining or losing, it is impossible to improve your health. Similarly, without detailed trade records, a trader cannot know what they are winning or losing, making improvement impossible.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Financial Wisdom TV – Trading for a Living by Dr Alexander Elder | https://www.financialwisdomtv.com/post/trading-for-a-living

Questions: What numerical records does Elder require for every trade?
Answers: For every trade, Elder requires the trader to record: entry price, exit price, entry and exit dates, number of shares, long or short, commissions, exchange fees, gross and net profit or loss, source of the trade idea, analytical method used, and the Buy, Sell, and Trade Grades.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: What do the 16 traders in 'Entries and Exits' have in common according to Elder?
Answers: After the book Entries and Exits was published, people kept asking Elder what the 16 featured traders had in common. Despite their differences in style, market, and timeframe — men and women, long-term and short-term, stocks and futures, American and foreign — they all kept excellent records.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: How does Elder use trade grading to improve risk management?
Answers: By grading each trade's Buy, Sell, and Trade quality numerically, Elder creates a measurable performance dataset. A trader who consistently scores low Buy Grades is paying too much on entry — reducing the risk-reward ratio before the trade even begins. Grading reveals execution problems that would otherwise remain hidden in overall P&L numbers.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: What is the relationship between record-keeping and the equity curve?
Answers: The equity curve is built from the cumulative results recorded in the trading spreadsheet. Without accurate records, the equity curve cannot be drawn accurately. The equity curve is the summary output of all risk decisions; the records are the inputs that make it meaningful.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder mean when he says records 'make you your own teacher'?
Answers: Elder uses this phrase throughout his books to convey that trading improvement comes from self-directed learning — analysing your own past behaviour, identifying patterns, and adjusting. No external teacher can observe every trade you take; your records are the only comprehensive record of your own strengths, weaknesses, and blind spots.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does the trading journal complement the spreadsheet in Elder's system?
Answers: The spreadsheet captures numerical data — prices, sizes, grades, P&L. The trading journal captures the qualitative dimension — the trader's reasoning at the time of entry, their emotional state, their concerns, and their post-trade assessment. Together they create a complete picture that allows both analytical and psychological review.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: Why does Elder say poor record-keeping is a form of self-deception?
Answers: Traders who keep no records avoid confronting the reality of their performance. They can maintain the illusion that they are doing better than they actually are, or attribute losses to bad luck rather than bad decision-making. Elder says this is the trading equivalent of an alcoholic not counting how many drinks they've had.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the frequency of reviewing trading records?
Answers: Elder recommends reviewing records regularly — ideally after every trade and again at the end of each week and month. The weekly review monitors the progression toward the 6% monthly limit; the monthly review analyses the equity curve and grades to identify systemic patterns in both wins and losses.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does tracking slippage in records contribute to risk management?
Answers: Elder notes that the average trader spends three times more on slippage than on commissions — yet most traders never track it. Recording the price at order placement versus the fill price reveals the true cost of execution, allowing the trader to adjust their entry timing, switch to limit orders, or choose more liquid instruments to reduce slippage.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder mean by the fourth pillar that ties the Three Ms together?
Answers: Elder identifies record-keeping as the fourth factor that integrates Mind, Method, and Money. Psychology tells you how to manage yourself; method tells you what to trade; money management tells you how much to risk. But without records, there is no feedback loop connecting these three pillars — no way to learn what is working and what is not.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is the 'Iron Triangle' and how does record-keeping fit into it?
Answers: Elder's Iron Triangle of risk control consists of three mutually reinforcing elements: discipline (following rules consistently), risk control (the 2% and 6% Rules), and record-keeping (the trade journal and equity curve). Remove any one side and the structure collapses. Records are not optional — they are architecturally essential.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Grokipedia – The New Trading for a Living | https://grokipedia.com/page/the_new_trading_for_a_living_psychology_discipline_trading_tools_and_systems_risk_control_trade_mana

Questions: How does Elder's concept of 'A-trade' relate to record-keeping?
Answers: An A-trade is one that meets all of Elder's entry criteria across every screen and satisfies the Iron Triangle risk parameters. Keeping records of which trades were classified as A-trades — and tracking their actual outcomes versus B- or C-trades — allows the trader to verify empirically that their highest-conviction setups actually outperform lower-quality ones.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about traders who throw away brokerage statements?
Answers: Elder uses the image of traders who throw away brokerage statements and keep no records as a direct analogy to alcoholics who refuse to count their drinks. Both are engaged in denial — avoiding the confrontation with reality that accurate tracking would force. He says this behaviour is a hallmark of losing traders.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does the daily homework routine support risk management?
Answers: Elder's daily homework — reviewing charts and updating records at the end of each trading day — ensures that the equity curve is current, open risk is accurately tallied against the 6% limit, and the visual diary is populated before memories fade. This discipline prevents small oversights from compounding into systemic blind spots.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is position sizing and why is it central to Elder's risk framework?
Answers: Position sizing is the process of determining how many shares, contracts, or lots to trade on each position. Elder considers it central because the same entry signal executed with the wrong position size can produce either an acceptable loss or a devastating one. The Iron Triangle formula — maximum risk ÷ risk per share — makes position sizing mathematical rather than discretionary.
Label: Risk Management
Source: SteadyOptions – How Position Sizing Impacts Your Returns | https://steadyoptions.com/articles/position-sizing/

Questions: How does the Iron Triangle formula calculate position size?
Answers: Position size = (2% of account equity) ÷ (entry price − stop-loss price). The numerator is the maximum dollar risk allowed by the 2% Rule; the denominator is the per-share risk defined by the distance from entry to stop. Dividing the first by the second gives the maximum number of shares to trade.
Label: Risk Management
Source: StockCharts ChartWatchers – Dr Elder Joins ChartWatchers | https://stockcharts.com/articles/chartwatchers/2010/05/dr-alexander-elder-joins-chartwatchers.html

Questions: What happens to position size when the stop is set tighter?
Answers: A tighter stop means less risk per share. With less risk per share and the same 2% dollar risk limit, the Iron Triangle formula produces a larger permitted position size. Tighter stops allow larger positions — which is why securing a high-quality entry near support is so important in Elder's framework.
Label: Risk Management
Source: A Discounted View – Managing Position Level Risk with Elder's 2% Rule | http://adiscountedview.blogspot.com/2016/03/managing-position-level-risk-with-dr.html

Questions: What happens to position size when the stop is set wider?
Answers: A wider stop means more risk per share. The same 2% dollar risk limit divided by a higher per-share risk produces a smaller permitted position size. Wide stops force the trader into small positions — which is the correct outcome, because a wide stop reflects greater uncertainty about the trade's immediate direction.
Label: Risk Management
Source: A Discounted View – Managing Position Level Risk with Elder's 2% Rule | http://adiscountedview.blogspot.com/2016/03/managing-position-level-risk-with-dr.html

Questions: Why does Elder say position sizing is the key differentiator between professionals and amateurs?
Answers: Elder observes that most amateurs spend approximately zero time on position sizing calculations, instead making impulsive size decisions based on recent trade outcomes. Professionals, by contrast, may spend up to a third of their research time on risk and position sizing calculations, treating it as an equally important input as the trade signal itself.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: How does improving entry quality affect position sizing under the 2% Rule?
Answers: Improving entry quality — buying closer to the low of the day, near a tight support level — reduces the distance to the logical stop-loss. A smaller entry-to-stop distance means less risk per share, which allows a larger position within the same 2% dollar risk limit. Elder's Iron Triangle rewards disciplined entries with larger permissible sizes.
Label: Risk Management
Source: StockCharts ChartWatchers – Dr Elder Joins ChartWatchers | https://stockcharts.com/articles/chartwatchers/2010/05/dr-alexander-elder-joins-chartwatchers.html

Questions: What is the relationship between the buy grade and risk management?
Answers: A high buy grade (buying near the low of the day's range) reduces the entry-to-stop distance, lowering per-share risk and allowing a larger position under the 2% Rule. A low buy grade (buying near the high of the day) widens the entry-to-stop distance unnecessarily, forcing a smaller position and reducing the potential reward-to-risk ratio.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the sweet spot for a beginner's account size?
Answers: Elder recommends that beginners aim for an account in the $20,000 to $50,000 range. This range is large enough to diversify across multiple positions and absorb normal losses without immediate ruin, yet small enough that the dollar amounts involved do not create overwhelming psychological pressure or a false sense of security.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: What is the risk of trading a very small account under the 2% Rule?
Answers: With a very small account — say $5,000 — 2% equals only $100 per trade. On most stocks with reasonable stop distances, this permits buying only a handful of shares. The resulting positions may be too small to diversify effectively and the trading costs (commissions and slippage) may consume a disproportionate share of any gains.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: How does the 2% Rule interact with diversification?
Answers: If every trade risks 2% and the 6% Rule limits total monthly risk to 6%, the trader can hold at most three simultaneous positions at full 2% risk. This natural constraint promotes diversification by preventing the trader from concentrating too heavily in any single position or sector.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about reducing position size after a losing streak?
Answers: Elder recommends cutting position size — potentially to the minimum — after a series of losses, especially as the 6% monthly limit approaches. Smaller positions reduce financial exposure while allowing the trader to stay engaged with the market, rebuild confidence, and diagnose the cause of the losing streak without risking further significant damage.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder use channel width to evaluate trade quality?
Answers: Elder divides the daily channel — the vertical distance between the upper and lower envelope lines — into a benchmark for trade quality. A trade that captures more than 30% of the channel's width earns an A grade. This metric evaluates whether the trader is entering at the right point in the channel to capture a meaningful portion of the expected price movement.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: What does Elder mean by 'don't swing for the fences'?
Answers: Elder advises against trying to capture the maximum possible gain from every trade. Setting realistic, achievable targets — capturing 30–40% of the channel rather than trying to catch the absolute top — produces more consistent results and fewer trades that are held too long and give back profits while waiting for an improbable extreme.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: How does Elder approach the decision to scale out of a position?
Answers: Elder sometimes exits a position in two or more instalments — selling the first portion when the initial profit target is reached and leaving the rest to run with a trailing stop. This approach locks in a portion of the gain while preserving exposure to further upside, balancing the competing demands of capital protection and profit maximisation.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: What is the risk-reward ratio and how does Elder use it?
Answers: The risk-reward ratio compares the potential profit of a trade (distance from entry to target) to the potential loss (distance from entry to stop). Elder seeks trades where the potential reward is at least twice the risk — a 2:1 ratio — as a minimum standard. Higher ratios (3:1 or better) are preferred, particularly with ATR-based targets.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Grokipedia – The New Trading for a Living | https://grokipedia.com/page/the_new_trading_for_a_living_psychology_discipline_trading_tools_and_systems_risk_control_trade_mana

Questions: How does the ATR help define both stops and targets simultaneously?
Answers: The ATR measures a security's typical daily price range. Elder uses multiples of the ATR to set both stops (entry minus 2 × ATR for longs) and targets (entry plus 3 × ATR or more). This creates a built-in minimum 1.5:1 risk-reward ratio and ensures that both the stop and target are calibrated to the security's actual volatility.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Grokipedia – The New Trading for a Living | https://grokipedia.com/page/the_new_trading_for_a_living_psychology_discipline_trading_tools_and_systems_risk_control_trade_mana

Questions: What does Elder say about the relationship between the reward-to-risk ratio and win rate?
Answers: Elder teaches that a trading system with a 3:1 reward-to-risk ratio can be profitable even with a win rate well below 50%. For example, a system that wins only 40% of the time but makes three times as much on winners as it loses on losers will produce a positive expectancy over time — demonstrating why risk-reward discipline matters as much as raw win rate.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Grokipedia – The New Trading for a Living | https://grokipedia.com/page/the_new_trading_for_a_living_psychology_discipline_trading_tools_and_systems_risk_control_trade_mana

Questions: What does Elder say about a beginner who trades to learn versus to earn?
Answers: Elder explicitly frames early trading as an educational investment rather than an income-generating activity. He compares it to dental or engineering school — you would not expect to earn a living from it in the first year. He advises beginners to trade minimum size, keep rigorous records, and focus on learning correct process rather than maximising profit.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: How does Elder's approach to position sizing differ from simply using a fixed lot size?
Answers: A fixed lot size ignores both the trader's current account equity and the specific risk of each trade. Elder's Iron Triangle approach dynamically adjusts position size based on actual account equity and the current trade's stop distance. This produces consistent dollar risk across all trades regardless of how different the setups or securities are.
Label: Risk Management
Source: A Discounted View – Managing Position Level Risk with Elder's 2% Rule | http://adiscountedview.blogspot.com/2016/03/managing-position-level-risk-with-dr.html

Questions: How does Elder account for slippage in position sizing?
Answers: Elder recommends including an allowance for slippage in the risk calculation, since the actual exit price when a stop is triggered may be slightly worse than the stop level — particularly in fast markets. Including a realistic slippage estimate in the per-share risk calculation ensures the true dollar risk remains within the 2% cap even in adverse conditions.
Label: Risk Management
Source: BWTS Ask Colin – Could you explain the 6% Rule | https://www.bwts.com.au/resources/ask-colin/817-could-you-explain-the-6-rule-as-opposed-to-the-2-rule-and-sugges.html

Questions: What is the risk of over-diversifying under Elder's framework?
Answers: While diversification reduces concentration risk, over-diversifying spreads the trader's attention too thinly. Elder recommends maintaining a manageable watchlist — ideally reviewing no more than five charts daily with full attention rather than twenty charts occasionally. Over-diversification also fragments position sizes to the point where transaction costs become a significant percentage of each trade.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: How does Elder handle the risk in leveraged instruments like futures and forex?
Answers: In leveraged instruments, Elder applies the same 2% and 6% Rules but uses the actual dollar value of the contract to calculate per-unit risk — not the margin required. He warns against overleveraging in volatile conditions and recommends reducing position sizes when implied volatility rises, to keep actual dollar risk within the 2% cap.
Label: Risk Management
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the relationship between account size and psychological pressure?
Answers: Elder observes that very small accounts create pressure to overtrade (to recover losses quickly) while very large accounts create a false sense of security that can lead to underdisciplined risk management. His recommended $20,000–$50,000 beginner range occupies a psychological sweet spot where losses are consequential enough to teach discipline but not so large as to cause panic.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Step by Step Trading – Elder (2015, elder.com) | https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/elderdownload/wp/wp-content/uploads/20180314155306/Step_by_Step_Trading_Elder.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about staging into a position?
Answers: Elder supports building a position in stages — entering an initial position when the setup qualifies, and adding to it on confirmation (such as a second pullback or an improved entry) — as long as the combined risk of all increments still satisfies the 2% Rule. Staging in allows a larger final position with a lower average entry price.
Label: Risk Management
Source: BWTS Ask Colin – Could you explain the 6% Rule | https://www.bwts.com.au/resources/ask-colin/817-could-you-explain-the-6-rule-as-opposed-to-the-2-rule-and-sugges.html

Questions: How does the Impulse System affect position sizing decisions?
Answers: Elder uses the Impulse System as a position sizing filter: full-size positions are reserved for green-bar (fully bullish) conditions; reduced positions may be taken on blue-bar (neutral) conditions; no long positions are taken on red-bar conditions. This systematically scales exposure to the strength of the prevailing trend signal.
Label: Risk Management
Source: Grokipedia – The New Trading for a Living | https://grokipedia.com/page/the_new_trading_for_a_living_psychology_discipline_trading_tools_and_systems_risk_control_trade_mana

Questions: What is the risk of adding to a losing position?
